coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanoprobe derived from Nyctanthes arbor-tristis flower: Unveiling the surface defect-derived fluorescence
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Pansari, Pratibha
Durga, Geeta
Sharma, Roopali
coredata.dc:description: Dual Emissive (green and blue) Carbon dots (C-Dots) aka g-CD and b-CD were synthesized using flowers of Nyctanthes arbortristis as the sole precursor via hydrothermal method without the aid of any external passivating agent. In the present report, the effect of time and temperature on the hydrothermal reaction was evaluated in order to modulate the surface defects that could lead to dual emissions. To gauge the nature, size, morphology, and optoelectronic characteristics, the C-Dots were characterized using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–Vis spectroscopy and Fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescence studies of both the Carbon Dots revealed their excitation-dependent emission characteristics with the bathochromic shift. Furthermore, both g-CD and b-CD could effectively be utilized as efficient fluorescent probes for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+. These fluorescent nanoprobes could selectively detect Fe3+ over a wide range of concentrations (3 µM to 100 µM) with limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.06 µM and 0.70 µM respectively. These tuneable Carbon Dots having wider solubilities would open a new avenue as Nanosensors for real-time applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nyctanthes arbortristis flower
Surface defects
Fluorescence
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon Dots as nanoprobe for selective and sensitive detection of ferric ion (Fe3+)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Central Instrumentation Facility Jamia Millia Islamia

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research work was financially supported by Sharda University. Author P Pansari gratefully acknowledges Sharda University for providing the research fellowship (SU/RES/SBSR/Chemistry/2021212714/515). The authors acknowledge Central Instrumentation Facility Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi and Mr Taufail Ahmed, in charge CIF, JMI for providing their instrumentation facility to carried out emission studies. We acknowledge Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility, IIT-Delhi for HR-TEM and Material Research Centre MNIT Jaipur for XPS facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS Data: (a) XPS survey spectrum of g-CD, (b) XPS survey spectrum of b-CD, (c) C (1 s), (d) O (1 s) (e) N (1 s) spectra of g-CD, (e) C (1 s), (f) O (1 s) (g) N (1 s) spectra of b-CD.
(a) FT-IR Spectra of g-CD and b-CD (b) TEM image of g-CD (c) TEM image of b-CD.
UV–vis spectra of g-CD and b-CD on 175 °C and 200 °C for 16 and 24 h.
PL spectra of g-CD and b-CD on different excitation (a) g-CD at 175 °C for 16 h (b) g-CD at 175 °C for 24 h (c) b-CD at 200 °C for 16 h (d) b-CD at 200 °C for 24 h.
Photo stability of (a) g-CD and (b) b-CD.
PL Decay curve of (a) g-CD and b-CD (b) g-CD + Fe3+ and (c) b-CD + Fe3+.
PL response of Carbon Dots in the presence of different metal salt (a) g-CD (b) b-CD.
PL response (F/F0) of Carbon Dot in the presence of different metal salt (a) g-CD (b) b-CD.
Effect of different concentrations of Fe3+ salt on PL emission (a) g-CD (b) b-CD.
Effect of common metal ions interference on PL emission: (a) g-CD (b) b-CD and Selectivity investigation (F0/F) on PL emission of CDs (3 µM–18 µM) (c) g-CD (d) b-CD.
Hydrothermal Synthesis of g-CD and b-CD.

Green and Blue emissive CDs: (a) and (b) g-CD in Day light for 16 h and 24 h (c) and (d) b-CD in Day light for 16 h and 24 h, (e) and (f) g-CD in UV light for 16 h and 24 h, (g) and (h) b-CD in UV light for 16 h and 24 h.
Schematic representation of different electronic transitions in g-CD and b-CD.
Optical Characterization study of g-CD and b-CD.
Comparison of the analytical performance of different CDs for Fe3+ sensing and QY.
Detection of Fe3+ in Real Samples.
Comparison of the analytical performance of different CDs for Fe3+ sensing in Real samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanoprobe derived from Nyctanthes arbor-tristis flower: Unveiling the surface defect-derived fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, School of Basic Sciences and Research, Sharda University, Greater Noida 201306, U.P., India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Basic Sciences and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, School of Basic Sciences and Research, Sharda University, Greater Noida 201306, U.P., India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual Emissive (green and blue) Carbon dots (C-Dots) aka g-CD and b-CD were synthesized using flowers of Nyctanthes arbortristis as the sole precursor via hydrothermal method without the aid of any external passivating agent. In the present report, the effect of time and temperature on the hydrothermal reaction was evaluated in order to modulate the surface defects that could lead to dual emissions. To gauge the nature, size, morphology, and optoelectronic characteristics, the C-Dots were characterized using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–Vis spectroscopy and Fluorescence spectroscopy. The fluorescence studies of both the Carbon Dots revealed their excitation-dependent emission characteristics with the bathochromic shift. Furthermore, both g-CD and b-CD could effectively be utilized as efficient fluorescent probes for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+. These fluorescent nanoprobes could selectively detect Fe3+ over a wide range of concentrations (3 µM to 100 µM) with limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.06 µM and 0.70 µM respectively. These tuneable Carbon Dots having wider solubilities would open a new avenue as Nanosensors for real-time applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The serendipitous discovery of Carbon dots (C-Dots) led to the opening of new horizons in the domain of fluorescent nanoparticles [1]. C-Dots (CDs) are water-soluble nanoparticles with an ultrafine size (<10 nm) which have been visualized as an alternative to conventional semiconductor quantum dots and found applications in the area of chemical sensing, biosensing, medical diagnosis, bioimaging, photovoltaic devices, and photocatalysis [2].

Thitherto, a number of methods under the broad classification of Top-down and Bottom-up approach such as microwave-assisted method, sol–gel method, pyrolysis, solvothermal, hydrothermal, etc. have been employed to synthesize these mystic stars [3]. However, regardless of the technique being utilized to synthesize them, the C-Dots appear to be quasispherical, ranging from amorphous to crystalline, and have a graphene-like sp2 carbon structure [4]. A typical Carbon Dot is characterized by the presence of functional groups such as hydroxyl groups (−OH), carboxyl groups (−COOH), and amino groups (−NH2) on the surface of the C=C carbon core depending upon the source [5]. The same can be exhibited in the absorption spectrum of C-Dots where an n-π* transition of the C = O bond (∼300 nm) and the π-π* transition of the aromatic C–C bonds (∼230 nm) are observed as strong absorption bands in the UV region [6]. The physicochemical properties of Carbon Dots such as tuneable photoluminescence, magnificent photostability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and excellent water solubility are attributed to them owing to the flexibility of their surface passivation and surface functionalization [5 7]. However, the issues related to some of the synthetic processes such as high-temperature requirements, the need for particular equipment, use of toxic solvents, inorganic/organic molecules, stabilizing agents, synthetic capping agents, and expensive precursors, the demand for simplistic approaches is the matter of concern. Sometimes the toxicity is also exhibited by traditionally synthesized C-Dots which obstruct their multifaceted application and give a call for a more facile synthesis method [8].

The green approach of C-Dots synthesis where the precursor is of natural origin may be proven as an alternative to all the above issues along with being cost-effective as well as eco-friendly [9]. The fringe benefits of this approach are its research reproducibility and scalability, which have shifted the focus of researchers over the last few years to synthesize natural source-derived fluorescent C-Dots [10]. In the synthesis of majority of natural source-derived C-Dots, the hydrothermal method has been employed due to its ease of operation [11]. The rich and diverse presence of phytochemicals and secondary metabolites in plant-based natural sources make them the preferred choice of researchers for C-Dots synthesis [12,13]. The different phytochemicals present in plant parts such as seeds, fruit, flowers, and leaves provide structural diversity and lead to carbonization under different reaction conditions to form carbon core i.e., the skeleton as well as capping agent or passivating agents of C-Dots. These organic moieties or self-functionalizing/ passivating agents have made it possible to achieve stable C-Dots with an abundant function group without the use of any synthetic external precursor or capping agent [14]. Pourreza et. al., synthesized Carbon Dots from Prosopis juliflora leaves which exhibited a blue luminescence by the rapid one-pot process [15]. Wang et al. have synthesized C-Dots from Osmanthus fragrans Lour for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ with an 18.53% Quantum Yield by hydrothermal method [16]. Arumugham et. al. prepared fluorescent C-Dots using Catharanthus roseus [17]. Elaeagnus Angustifolia has been used to synthesize C-Dots with 16.8% Quantum Yield by hydrothermal method [18]. It is well evident from the literature that the different types of precursors led to different functional groups on the surface which gave structural diversity, high solubility in a polar solvent, and surface defects at different energy levels leading to a number of electronic transitions [19 20 21]. The different surface states and defects of C-Dots are the dominating factors in establishing their luminescent property. The chemical structure and thus photophysical behaviour of C-Dots can be modulated by the wise selection of starting material [22] and by optimizing the reaction conditions [23].

Further, it is always difficult to achieve C-Dots with different emissions in the visible spectra from the same starting material without the use of any external precursor. In the proposed work, we have taken it into account to develop the synthesis strategy to obtain dual emissive C-Dots in an aqueous solution without the use of any external precursor with the aim of studying all these unresolved explanations. To the best of our knowledge, no one has reported dual colour emissions from the same starting material without the use of any external precursor or in/organic additive. We have used Nyctanthes arbortristis flowers as the starting material or the skeleton of C-Dots for the synthesis of dual emissive CDs.

Nyctanthes arbortristis flower (N. Arbortristis) is commonly known as Night jasmine, Harsinghar or Parijat flower and belongs to the family Oleaceae. The flower is made up of white petals and orange tubular calyx [24]. It contains different bioactive compounds such as apigenin, anthocyanin, tannin, D-mannitol, glucose, quercetin, carotenoid, glycosides, nyctanthin, kaemferol, etc [25]. and has been used for treating fevers, appetite, arthritis, obstinate sciatica, liver disorders, biliary disorders, intestinal worms traditionally [26].

In our laboratory, we have synthesized green (g-CD) and blue color (b-CD) emissive C-Dots by hydrothermal method using Nyctanthes arbortristis flowers as the precursor. In this work, we have investigated the effect of time and temperature on hydrothermal reactions to achieve dual emissive C-Dots by virtue of surface defects at different energy levels. Furthermore, the surface morphologies of C-Dots were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and their chemical composition was studied by the X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectroscopy, and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The optical properties of C-Dots were studied by UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. The as-synthesized and characterized C-Dots were successfully used for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+.

Nyctanthes arbortristis flowers were collected from the local garden. The salts for Al3+ , As3+, Ca2+ Cu2+, Pb2+, Ti4+, Fe3+ , K+, Cr3+, and Zn2+ were purchased from Merck & Co. L-Tryptophan was purchased from HiMedia Laboratories Pvt Ltd. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received. The Milli-Q water was used to make the solutions throughout the experiments.

To affirm the structural and optoelectronic characteristics of newly synthesized green emissive (g-CD) and blue emissive C-Dots (b-CD), various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques were utilized. Omicron Nanotechnology X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) has been used to study the elemental composition of C-Dots. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) by Agilent, Cary 630 FTIR-ATR was utilized to analyze the sample in the range from 4000 to 600 cm−1. ZETASIZER Nano-ZSP of Marvel has been used to study the charge on dispersed phase using quartz cells. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) of the JEOL 2100 (USA) operating at 200 kV and 1,000,000X magnification was used to obtain the TEM images. Cu-grid has been used to image the g-CD and b-CD. UV–Vis absorption spectra were acquired using a Shimadzu UV1800 spectrophotometer while taking the solution in a two-sided optically clear quartz cell of 10 mm path length. Fluorescence emission spectra of the samples were recorded with help of a Fluorescence spectrometer (Carry Eclipse, Agilent, USA) using four sides of optically clear quartz cell of 10 mm of path length while keeping the slit width of 5 mm for excitation and emission. To explore the fluorescence properties of CDs in detail, the excitation wavelength was altered from 240 nm to 400 nm at an interval of 20 nm. Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) was used to determine the fluorescence lifetime. The fluorescence decay of g-CD and b-CD were recorded with the help of the Horiba DeltaFlex01-DD time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer.

In a typical synthesis procedure, g-CDs and b-CD were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal process as illustrated in Scheme 1 . 1.5 g of dried flowers in 48 ml Milli-Q water were placed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 ml volume) for hydrothermal treatment. A number of samples were prepared while maintaining the temperature at 175 °C and 200 °C for 16 and 24 h. The autoclave was then allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. After each reaction, the brown solution was obtained and was later purified. These solutions were passed through a three-step purification process, firstly filtered using Whatman filter paper followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and further, the supernatant solution was filtered by syringe filter (0.2 µm). The resultant aqueous solution was stored at 4 °C for further studies.

The quantum yield of obtained fluorescent C-Dots has been evaluated by the reference method for which we have used L-Tryptophan (Q.Y. = 0.13 ± 0.01) as standard reference. The absorbance of the standard and the sample were kept at 0.1 in a 10 mm fluorescence cuvette to minimize the inner filter effect. Absorbance and emission spectra of all the samples were recorded at 0.1 absorbances.
φ
CD
=
φ R
×
I
CD
I R
×
OD
R
OD
CD
×
η
CD
2
η
R
2

The given equation was used to calculate the relative fluorescence quantum yield of g-CD and b-CD. Where R and CD stand for reference and sample, φ denotes the fluorescence quantum yield. I is the integrated fluorescence intensity v/s absorbance, OD is the optical density and η is the refractive index of solvent.

Time-Resolved PL measurements were performed to study the life time of g-CD and b-CD. The decay trace has fitted using the following tri-exponential function on the basis of nonlinear least square analysis.
Y
t
= A +
B 1
e x p
t
τ 1
+
B 2
e x p
t
τ 2
+
B 3
e x p
t
τ 3

The B1, B2 and B3 are corresponding amplitudes where the τ1, τ2, and τ3 are the different decay times. The time-resolved PL measurements were done using the Ludox solution for the reference at the 284 nm excitation wavelength of the LED source and the data value was calculated by using Ez software.

The newly synthesized fluorescent C-Dots have been utilized as a fluorescence nanoprobe for the detection of metal ions in water samples. In a typical metal sensing procedure, the stock solutions of 300 µM concentration for different metal salts (Al3+, As3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, K+, Ti4+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Zn2+) were prepared using Milli-Q water. The diluted CDs were taken in the quartz cuvette to record the maximum fluorescence intensity F0.

The hydrothermal method reminiscent of the bottom-up approach of C-Dots synthesis was utilized to synthesize them from Nyctanthes arbor-tristis flower in the current investigation. In order to comprehend the role of precursors and diverse PL centres of carbon dots [27], we carried out various reactions at different times and temperatures.

Under the controlled condition of temperature and time, the precursors underwent a series of events such as reduction, co-condensation, and carbonization phase [28] and resulted in dual emissive C-Dots. The C-Dots synthesized at 175 °C (16/24 hrs) and 200 °C (16/24 hrs) in an aqueous solution were pale brown under daylight and glowed green and blue respectively when irradiated by a UV lamp of 365 nm (Scheme 1 and 2 ). We speculate that the formation of dual emissive carbon dots from the same starting material even without the use of any in/organic additive could be attributed to the different particle sizes and multiple PL centres. However, the degree of carbonization could be the anchor of multiple PL centres formed at different temperature conditions and the variation of 25 °C resulted in the increased core size of the C-Dots.

In order to understand the elemental constituents of g-CD and b-CD, we employed the XPS technique. XPS study (Fig. 1 ) showed that both the g-CD and b-CD contained C, N, and O elements and no trace of additional elements in the survey graph which highlighted the purity of CDs. The binding energies of g-CD were observed to be 282.9, 399, 530.2 eV and for b-CDs the values were observed to be 282.2, 398.6, 530.3 eV. The high-resolution C(1s) XPS spectrum of g-CD exhibited characteristic peaks at 284, 285.6, and 287 eV, which corresponded to C–C/C = C, C-N, and C = O/C = N respectively. N(1s) exhibited a peak at 399.2 which corresponded to C-N-C and O(1s) exhibited a peak at 531.8 and 534.2 which corresponded to C-O and O–H.

Furthermore, the high-resolution C(1s) XPS spectrum of b-CD exhibited characteristic peaks at 284, 286, and 288 eV which corresponded to C–C/C = C, C-O, and C = O/C = N. N(1s) exhibited peaks at 399.2 which corresponded to C-N-C and O(1 s) exhibited peaks at 531.8 and 534.2 which corresponded to C-O and O–H.

The FTIR spectra were acquired to identify the functional groups present on the surface and core of both the CDs as indicated in Fig. 2 . The strong and broad absorption band in the range of 3600–3000 cm−1 was obtained in both g-CD and b-CD and has been attributed to the presence of the O–H functional group. The characteristic peaks of C–H stretching vibrations were observed at 2916 cm−1 and 2921 cm−1 for g-CD and b-CD respectively. The peak at 1588 cm−1 and 1592 cm−1, corresponded to stretching vibrations of the carbonyl group (C = O/C = N) of g-CD and b-CD. The peaks at 1389 cm−1 for g-CD and 1386 cm−1 for b-CD are most likely related to the asymmetrical and symmetrical deformation vibration of a methyl group attached to an aromatic ring. The peak at 1205 cm−1 in g-CD and 1208 cm−1 in b-CD spectra vouched for C-N whereas 1040 cm−1 corresponded to C–O stretching in both cases. These results thus indicated the presence of phenolic –OH/–COOH groups on the surface of g-CD and b-CD. However, the source of nitrogen was nitrogen-bearing heterocycles that might have become part of the core of C-Dots [29]. Hence, the FTIR and XPS data demonstrated the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface and the negative value of zeta potential accomplished the result that the surface of both C-Dots has negatively charged moieties which endowed their excellent water solubility for a longer duration.
Further, the measured pH value of g-CD and the b-CD solution was 3.4 and 3.6 respectively which is indicative of the presence of –OH & –COOH functional groups on the surface of carbon dots.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) imaging technique was used to determine the size of carbon dots. The average core size of C-Dots was determined by the particle size distribution plot (Fig. 2), selecting 42 dots, which revealed that they have an average diameter of 3.58 nm and 2.00 nm for g-CD and b-CD. As per the HRTEM result, the greater size of g-CD as compared to b-CD concluded that the degree of carbonization reduced the size of C-Dots.

The structural and optical characterization results thus demonstrated that the degree of carbonization increased as the reaction temperature increased. The size of the carbon core increased at the constant temperature while the time was increased, and the functional group decreased hence photoluminescence of C-Dots exhibited possibilities of different electronic transitions (as shown in Scheme 3 ). This in-depth study of the structure and PL optimization would open new ways to synthesize efficient systems to be utilized in bioscience, bioimaging, and energy technology [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon Dots as nanoprobe for selective and sensitive detection of ferric ion (Fe3+)
ce:section.ce:para:
A limited amount of metal ions is crucial for the upkeep of all sorts of life and excess of these ions may lead to health and environmental hazards. Therefore, it becomes important to sense their amount in the given sample to keep the check [39]. However, selective and sensitive detection of metal is pivotal. A number of metal ion detection sensors have been published and patented in the last few decades and C-Dots are a relatively new entry [40]. The use of nanosensors is considered to be advantageous as they represent the sensitive result for metal or molecular detection even at the trace level [40]. With their chemical inertness, high surface/volume ratio, rapid detection ability, less toxicity, and unique optoelectronic properties, they are considered to be fit for colorimetric or fluorescence-based sensors when compared to other nanomaterials [41]. In the present study, the g-CD and b-CD were tested for their metal ion testing ability. The metal salt solutions of 300 µM concentration of the metals under study viz. Al3+, As3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, K1+, Ti4+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Zn2+ were prepared. Two experimental setups i.e., for g-CD and b-CD were designed by diluting each of the metal ion solutions to 100 µl and then 10 µl of Carbon Dot solutions were poured into them individually followed by incubation for 5 min. The control experiment of only C-Dots solutions was also set up. The fluorescence spectra of each of the solutions were recorded at 281 nm and 275 nm for g-CD and b-CD respectively. We observed no change in the fluorescence intensity of C-Dots with any of the metal ions except for Fe3+ where the fluorescence of both g-CD and b-CD was significantly quenched (Figs. 7, 8
).

Furthermore, in order to find the limit of detection for Fe3+ ions, the Fe3+ solution was added systematically from 3 µM to 100 µM into the g-CD and b-CD (Figs. 8, 9 ). We observed a linear decrease in fluorescence intensity as we increased the concentration of Fe3+ ions ranging from 3 µM to 100 µM. We employed the Stern–Volmer equation to evaluate the fluorescence quenching caused by Fe3+ ions.
F 0
F
- 1 =
K
SV
Q

Where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the C-Dots without Fe3+ ions, whereas F represents fluorescence intensity with Fe3+ ions, Q is the concentration of Fe3+ and KSV for green and blue CD is 0.0535 and 0.0264 (slope of the linear fit) is a quenching constant (Fig. 10 ). This equation represents a linear relationship between (F0/F) and Fe3+ concentration with R2 values of 0.9823 and 0.7384 for g-CD and b-CD. The limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ ion was calculated by the following equation:
LimitofDetection =
3 σ
K

Where σ is the standard deviation and K is the slope of linear range i.e., 3 µM to 18 µM. The calculated LOD values for g-CD and b-CD are 0.06 µM and 0.70 µM respectively. The results are summarized in Table 3 and Table 4 .

In contrast to b-CD, g-CD showed a marked difference in its limit of detection ability which may be attributed to the presence of more trapping sites on the surface of g-CD as compared to b-CD. To understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Carbon Dots by metal ions, a few possible reasons have been proposed. Zhang et al. synthesized CQDs using polyoxyethylenebis (amine) (PEGdiamine) and citric acid via high-output solid-phase thermal method. They found out that the Fe3+ ion formed a complex with the hydroxyl group on the surface of CQDs and quenched the FL intensity [42]. Raveendran et al. derived Carbon Dots using mint leaves and unveiled that Fe3+ ions react with the hydroxyl groups of CDs to form the complex by special coordination [43].

It is assumed that the hydroxyl group at the sp2 carbon is necessary for the chelation with Fe3+ ion via non-radiative electron transfer [44]. Morphological and optoelectronic characterization of both CDs established the presence of more hydroxyl groups on the surface of g-CD as compared to b-CD. Thus, these hydroxyl groups induce stronger chelation to Fe3+ ion, owing to which the LOD of g-CD is lower than that of b-CD.

Further, when we added 3 µM Fe3+ in g-CD and b-CD the average lifetime decreased to 0.71 ns and 0.38 ns from 0.99 and 0.51 respectively and no significant change in lifetime after the addition of Fe3+ was observed (Fig. 6). It states that Fe3+ ignored the interaction with the excited state and thus confirmed the static quenching mechanism with the formation of a complex between fluorophore and Fe3+ in the ground state [45].

Real water samples i.e., Tap water and Drinking water were collected from the laboratory basin and water purifier. All the collected samples were centrifuged at 10,000 RPM for 10 min followed by syringe filtration using a 0.2 µm syringe filter to remove large particle impurities. Both real samples were spiked at different concentrations of Fe3+. The decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed with the increase in the spike concentration. To evaluate the recovery percent the following equation has been applied:
% R e c o v e r y =
C
spike
-
C
unspike
C
added
× 100 %

Cspike is the concentration of Fe3+ after the addition of standard Fe3+. Cunspike is the concentration before the addition of standard Fe3+ ions, and Cadded is the concentration of Fe3+ added in the real water sample. The recovery range for the g-CD in tap water was 96 to 103% and for drinking water was 94 to 103%. In the case of b-CD, the recovery range in tap water was 93 to 103% and in drinking water it was found in the range of 96 to 104%. The obtained results are discussed in Table 5 along with the comparative study in Table 6 using different Carbon Dots. These results show adequate accuracy and relevance towards Fe3+ ions detection in real samples.

This instructive work of synthesizing green and blue colour emissive C-Dots using Nyctanthes arbortristis flower without using any external precursor or passivating agent unravelled the surface defect-derived fluorescence. This work unravelled the relationships between PL and surface structures through semi quantitatively tuning oxygen functional groups on the surface of C-Dots. The proposed work elucidates that oxygen-containing functional groups as well as the carbon core have an astonishing influence on the photoluminescence behaviour of C-Dots and the photoluminescence property of C-Dots can be engineered with surface functional groups and C-Dots. The proposed Nano probe has been applied for sensitive detection of Fe3+ ion and showed sensitive detection of Fe3+ with 0.06 µM and 0.70 µM limit of detection for g-CD and b-CD respectively. In conclusion, these findings and adequate sensing study of both CDs on real samples pave the path for the multifaceted application in nanotronics.

Pratibha Pansari: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Geeta Durga: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision. Roopali Sharma: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was financially supported by Sharda University. Author P Pansari gratefully acknowledges Sharda University for providing the research fellowship (SU/RES/SBSR/Chemistry/2021212714/515). The authors acknowledge Central Instrumentation Facility Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi and Mr Taufail Ahmed, in charge CIF, JMI for providing their instrumentation facility to carried out emission studies. We acknowledge Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility, IIT-Delhi for HR-TEM and Material Research Centre MNIT Jaipur for XPS facility.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications
ACS Cent. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, R. Li, and B. Yang, “Carbon Dots: A New Type of Carbon-Based Nanomaterial with Wide Applications,” ACS Cent. Sci., vol. 6, no. 12, pp. 2179–2195, Dec. 2020, doi: 10.1021/ACSCENTSCI.0C01306.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications
ACS Cent. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, R. Li, and B. Yang, “Carbon Dots: A New Type of Carbon-Based Nanomaterial with Wide Applications,” ACS Cent. Sci., vol. 6, no. 12, pp. 2179–2195, 2020, doi: 10.1021/acscentsci.0c01306.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots from natural resource: A review
Mater. Today Chem.
